original_hypothesis.title: SgxVerifier Skips All Proof Verification During Contesting Allowing Unverified Contests
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns immediately without any verification when _ctx.isContesting is true. This means ANY address can contest an SGX-tier proof without actually possessing valid SGX attestation or signature. The contester only needs to post the contestBond (500 TKO on mainnet). While this is architecturally intentional (contests are economic bets), it means the SGX security guarantee does not apply to contests, reducing the bar for griefing attacks.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The contestable rollup design allows contests without full proof verification, but this means the cost of contesting a valid SGX proof is only the contestBond, not the cost of obtaining an actual SGX instance. This could incentivize speculative or griefing contests.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_ctx.isContesting) return; — skips all verification during contesting

preconditions: Block has been proven at SGX tier
Attacker has contestBond amount of TKO (500 TKO on mainnet)
Contester submits proof at same tier with different transition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early return on isContesting bypasses all SGX signature verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone with TKO can contest SGX proofs without any SGX capability
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No partial verification during contests; no contest-specific SGX check

analysis_questions: Is the contest bond sufficient to deter speculative contesting?
What is the expected cost/benefit of contesting every SGX proof?
Could systematic contesting delay block verification beyond acceptable thresholds?
validation_requirements: Calculate economic incentive: contestBond loss probability vs verification delay impact
Assess maximum number of blocks that can be simultaneously contested
Verify that higher-tier proof resolution correctly slashes invalid contesters

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Honest prover proves block at SGX tier with correct transition. 2) Attacker contests with arbitrary (wrong) transition, posting 500 TKO contestBond. 3) SgxVerifier.verifyProof skips verification (isContesting=true). 4) Block verification blocked until higher-tier proof resolves contest. 5) Attacker loses 3/4 of contestBond (375 TKO) when resolved, but delays verification by cooldown period of higher tier. 6) Repeat across many blocks to delay L2 finality.
prerequisites: Tolerance for loss of 375 TKO per invalid contest

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Griefing cost ~375 TKO/block; delays verification by hours per block
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users waiting for block finality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Guardian proofs can resolve; attacker eventually runs out of TKO

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No economic analysis of systematic contesting cost

validation_result.impact_summary: The early return in SgxVerifier.verifyProof during contesting is an intentional architectural decision of the contestable rollup design, not a code defect.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns immediately when _ctx.isContesting is true (line 139-140), bypassing SGX signature verification. However, this is explicitly by design. The contestable rollup architecture separates 'proving' from 'contesting' — contests are economic bets, not proof submissions. The code comment on line 138 states: 'Do not run proof verification to contest an existing proof'. LibProving.sol lines 161-175 further document this two-mode design: proving mode (higher tier) and contesting mode (same tier). The economic security model relies on contestBond slashing (75% burned) to deter frivolous contests, and higher-tier proof resolution to determine correctness. The contester must still pay the full contestBond (500 TKO on mainnet SGX tier) via tko.transferFrom in LibProving.sol, and loses 3/4 of it if wrong. This is not a missing safeguard but a deliberate design trade-off inherent to contestable rollups.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code at SgxVerifier.sol lines 138-139 explicitly returns early during contesting with a clear comment. LibProving.sol lines 161-175 document the two-mode verification design. LibProving.sol lines 217-229 implement the contest path with proper bond collection. The entire flow is architecturally consistent and intentional.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: NOT a vulnerability. The early return is intentional design for the contestable rollup model. Contests are economic bets secured by contestBond slashing, not proof-verified operations. The comment explicitly documents this intent.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls TaikoL1.proveBlock with a proof at the same tier as existing transition
2. LibProving.proveBlock determines isContesting = (_proof.tier == ts.tier && tier.contestBond != 0) at LibProving.sol:161
3. IVerifier.Context is constructed with isContesting=true at LibProving.sol:163-172
4. SgxVerifier.verifyProof is called, returns immediately at SgxVerifier.sol:139
5. Back in LibProving.proveBlock, since _proof.tier == ts.tier (not >), enters else branch at LibProving.sol:208
6. Contest path executes: tko.transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), tier.contestBond) at LibProving.sol:222
7. Transition is marked as contested with contester address and contestBond recorded
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The code path is NOT vulnerable. The contest bond collection at LibProving.sol:222 (tko.transferFrom for contestBond) provides the economic security mechanism. The contester loses 75% of their bond if the original proof is correct (resolved by higher-tier proof at LibProving.sol:260-264). This is the intended economic security model for the contestable rollup design.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: MainnetTierProvider.sol confirms SGX tier contestBond is 500 ether TKO. The economic parameters are hardcoded in the tier provider.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The griefing scenario described (contesting every block for ~375 TKO loss each) is a known economic attack vector inherent to ALL contestable rollup designs, not unique to this implementation. The protocol explicitly designs for this: (1) 75% of contest bond is burned to make griefing expensive, (2) Guardian proofs can resolve disputes as ultimate arbiter, (3) the attacker must have sufficient TKO to sustain the attack. The cost scales linearly with blocks contested while providing no profit to the attacker. This is an accepted design trade-off, not a code defect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No code defect exists. The behavior is intentional and documented. The economic parameters (500 TKO contestBond, 75% burn on loss) are the designed safeguards. The hypothesis itself acknowledges this is 'architecturally intentional.'
recommended_mitigations: No code fix needed - behavior is by design
Protocol could consider rate-limiting contests per address as an optional enhancement
Economic parameters (contestBond) can be adjusted via tier provider upgrades if griefing becomes problematic
validation_result.replication_plan: No replication needed - the code behavior is confirmed by reading and is working as designed.
uncertainties: The sufficiency of 500 TKO contestBond as a griefing deterrent depends on TKO market price, which is external to the code
additional_testing_needed: Economic simulation of sustained griefing campaigns to validate contestBond adequacy at various TKO price points
